next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

By the time Micah got to the school office, the back of his T-shirt was stuck to him from shoulder blade to waistband. August had that particular Miami meanness where the air did not move unless it was being paid to, and the front doors of the building were breathing out weak air-conditioning that smelled like copier heat and old floor wax. He stood by the trophy case and pretended to read the names etched into little brass plates from years before he was old enough to care. It was easier to look at dead football seasons than at the office window where Ms. Francis could see him waiting.

Marcia had sent him with bus fare, one extra dollar for a cold drink if the heat got at him, and exact instructions. Go to the office, pick up the packet, come straight back. Don't stop by the docks. Don't stop by anybody's house. Don't let Dre carry you nowhere stupid. At the sink she had counted the bills twice before folding them into his palm, her fingers cold from rinsing out somebody else's old yogurt container to save for leftovers. Behind her, Kerene had leaned over the hot plate and curled one eyelash with the back of a teaspoon because she still had not replaced the metal curler she swore TSA had stolen. Tiana had been digging through the dresser drawer that was not really hers anymore, moving her document sleeve from one stack of shirts to another like the papers might get legs and leave without her. The room had been full before breakfast. Micah had understood what Marcia meant without her saying it plain. The house did not have room for one more problem wearing hi...
open_hooks_to_carry: The landlord weaponizes procedure rather than shouting.
Elena's help is real but insufficient.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d earnings and the thing he had once raided to keep the house going, and finally saw it more clearly than he had while the shoulder tore. Sixty-five dollars did not begin to restore anything. It did not repair the folder, or the lie, or the job future that had narrowed with one bad reach on a broken slab. It barely covered the shape of what he had lost today.

Marcia tore an old pillowcase into a strip and made a sling because there was nothing else in the room meant for this kind of emergency. When she lifted his forearm into place, Delroy bit the inside of his cheek hard enough to taste blood. The fan clicked. Outside, a bus sighed to a stop and moved on. Tiana sat back down with the folder and the legal pad, but she did not start writing again. She kept glancing at the shoulder the way people checked a cracked window during storm season, not trusting it to stay as it was.

Marcia tucked the money into an envelope that had once held the light bill and wrote nothing on the front. No category matched. Not wages. Not savings. Not help.

"Tomorrow," she said, tightening the knot at the back of his neck, "you don't go nowhere lifting anything. You hear me?"

He let his head rest against the chair for one second. The apartment smelled of rice, bleach, onions, and concrete dust now, home and job ground together so fine there was no separating them.

"I hear."

The shoulder throbbed against the sling. On the table, under the white sheet of pad paper, the sixty-five dollars made a low untidy shape. It looked smaller every time he glanced at it, and the arm in his lap felt heavier.
state_deltas_required: Keep the family housed after losing Celia's income and Delroy's work capacity.
Kerene arrives with a folding cot, Tiana's room ceases to be hers, and privacy becomes another bill the family cannot pay.
